topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

I scrolled through the feed, a familiar, irritating buzz vibrating in my chest. Another perfectly curated sunset picture from 'Nova_Dreamer.' It was always the same—effortless joy, perfectly filtered light, a veneer of effortless existence that made my own mundane reality feel dull and clumsy. I clicked on her profile, expecting another stream of vapid updates, but the location tag below the latest post hit me like a physical blow: downtown. My city.

A slow, tight knot formed in my stomach. I reread the caption, a muscle twitching near my jaw. How could someone so utterly detached, so focused on this digital fantasy, actually inhabit the same concrete jungle I navigated every morning? I felt a sudden, sharp urge to delete the application, to sever this thread of irritating familiarity. I stared at the profile picture—a bright, wide smile—and let out a short, humorless exhale. The sheer audacity of it was almost laughable.

[story 2]

The notification popped up, demanding attention. It was Nova again, sharing a picture of a bustling street market. The vibrancy of the photo was almost offensively cheerful. I sighed, the sound escaping my lips as if releasing stale air. I tapped the link, my fingers hovering over the screen, a distinct feeling of annoyance rising in my throat. I had always viewed her online persona as a carefully constructed illusion, a distant, unattainable ideal. Now, the reality was slamming into my doorstep, smelling faintly of exhaust and stale coffee.

I pulled up the city map, zooming in on the coordinates she’d carelessly shared. It was undeniably my neighborhood. A strange, cold sensation washed over me, replacing the usual irritation with a distinct sense of being thoroughly unimpressed. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly across my chest. This wasn't a surprise; it was just another inconvenient fact about the world, another piece of noise I now had to filter out.

[story 3]

I watched the live story update. Nova was showing a shot of a recognizable landmark, one I passed every day on my commute. A flicker of something unpleasant—a tightening around my eyes—crossed my face. It was the realization that the ethereal figure I’d been interacting with was grounded, tethered to the same pavement as me. The contrast was jarring, almost insulting.

"Seriously?" I muttered under my breath, the word tasting sharp and brittle. I felt a sudden restlessness, a need to physically distance myself from this shared space, this accidental overlap. My hands clenched into fists on the desk, the knuckles turning white. There was no excitement, only a profound sense of being overlooked, as if her presence here only served to highlight how unremarkable my own existence felt in comparison.
